QF.id: VIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_34

question.text: If Trader A buys 10 Nifty futures contracts from Trader B (who is initiating a new short position), the open interest will:

answer.text: Increase by 10 contracts, because both A and B are opening new positions
Increase by 20 contracts, because 10 new longs and 10 new shorts are created
Remain unchanged, because one person's buy is another person's sell
Decrease by 10 contracts, because contracts are being traded and settled

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** When BOTH parties to a trade are initiating NEW positions (A opens a new long, B opens a new short), OI increases by the number of contracts traded (10). OI is one-sided, so we count 10 new open contracts, not 20.
OI changes: (1) Both new positions -> OI increases, (2) Both closing positions -> OI decreases, (3) One new, one closing -> OI unchanged.
**The Trap:** OI is counted one-sided (10, not 20). The statement "one person's buy is another's sell" is true but does not mean OI stays constant - it depends on whether positions are new or closing.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Understanding how different trade scenarios affect OI is a frequently tested concept in Series VIII.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** OI increases only when BOTH parties open new positions. OI decreases when BOTH close existing positions.
